evidence_excerpt: After cranio-cervical decompression with expansile duroplasty, she had immediate improvement and stable neurology over many months with regained weight, but continued to use a wheelchair for mobility.

quality_of_life.evidence_excerpt: Post-operatively she had immediate improvement in weakness and cranial nerve palsies, was soon tolerating a soft diet, and her neurology was stable over many months.

timeline_events.evidence_excerpt: In April 2007 she underwent fourth ventricular cysto–pleural shunt insertion.
At 4 months following surgery she deteriorated with left 7th nerve palsy, right 6th nerve palsy, tetraparesis, and difficulty swallowing.
A second shunt was placed in the new cyst in August 2007.
After the second operation the patient did not improve and developed increasing weakness and more marked bulbar disturbance.
The cysto–pleural shunt was clipped in December 2007.
Despite shunt clipping, her clinical state continued to deteriorate; she required naso-enteric feeding and developed marked tetraparesis.
In mid-April 2008 a posterior fossa and upper cervical canal decompression with expansile duroplasty was performed and the cysto–pleural shunt removed.
Post-operatively she had immediate improvement in weakness and 6th and 7th nerve palsies and was soon tolerating a soft diet.
Her motor function improved dramatically; follow-up MRI showed resolution of brainstem/cervical edema and decreasing syrinx.

notes_on_uncertainty: The exact month of diagnosis in 2006 is not specified; time periods are approximated relative to diagnosis. Long-term follow-up is described as "many months" post-decompression without precise duration beyond one year; functional limitation (wheelchair use) persists despite neurologic stabilization.
